payload.text:
Create a clean, academic-style, light-themed portfolio website for “Mahesh V” designed specifically for university professors and CSC Scholarship reviewers. When designing the site, prioritize clarity, professionalism, research orientation, and soft colors.

Use white backgrounds, soft blue or soft violet accents, minimalistic shadows, and a modern sans-serif font.
The website must look serious and academic — similar to a university research student page.

🎨 DESIGN REQUIREMENTS (LIGHT THEME)

Background: #FFFFFF or #FAFAFA

Accent Colors: soft blue (#4A90E2) OR soft violet (#7A6EE6)

Text colors: dark gray (#222), medium gray (#555)

Cards: rounded, white, subtle shadow

Spacing: large, breathable, calm

Vibe: professional, research-driven, polished

Responsive on desktop, tablet, mobile

No animations that look childish or playful

This must feel like a serious CS undergraduate who can join a lab.

📘 STRUCTURE OF THE WEBSITE
1. HERO SECTION

Include:

Name: Mahesh V

Navigation: About | Research Projects | Projects | Skills | Certifications | Contact

Headline: Future-Ready Technologist. Research-Driven Learner.

Subheadline: Building intelligent systems through curiosity, precision, and continuous learning.

Button: ▸ View My Research

Background should be clean, bright, minimal.

2. ABOUT SECTION (CSC-Optimized Tone)

Use this text exactly:

Mahesh V
Aspiring Computer Scientist | Human-Centered AI Enthusiast | Research-Oriented Developer

I’m a developer who builds projects to learn, not the other way around.
My journey includes creating intelligent systems like Solace, a memory-based AI companion, and the CerebroPulse Engine, a research prototype exploring timing-based neural behavior.
\nI am deeply interested in neural computation, cognitive modeling, and human-centered intelligent systems. Over the past two years, I have worked independently on full-stack applications, mobile development, and AI prototypes that align with these research directions.
\nI aim to pursue undergraduate studies in China, where I can contribute to research while strengthening my foundations in computer science and intelligent systems.

Tagline below:
“Precision, consistency, and purpose — my approach to technology and learning.”

3. RESEARCH PROJECTS SECTION (IMPORTANT FOR CSC)

Section Title: Research Projects

🧪 CerebroPulse Engine (Research Project)

A long-term experimental framework exploring timing-based neuron-like computation, adaptive signal behavior, and emotion-conditioned variation.
Designed to study simplified cognitive models and emergent behavior in artificial systems.

Stack: Python, NumPy, Matplotlib
Button: View Research

This must appear before regular projects.

4. FEATURED PROJECTS (LIGHT ACADEMIC STYLE)

Use clean white cards.

🧠 Solace

Memory-based AI companion for natural, emotionally aware conversations.
Stack: Python, HTML, JavaScript, GPT API

🌐 NihonPath (Language Learning Platform)

Simple language-learning app covering reading systems and vocabulary.
Stack: HTML, CSS, JavaScript

🏫 Campus Kizuna

Role-based communication platform for students & faculty.
Stack: Kotlin, Firebase

📘 Preparation Tracker

Daily study analytics and syllabus-tracking dashboard.
Stack: JavaScript, Express.js, Firebase

📝 LifeScript

AI-powered tool for generating structured chapters and eBooks.
Stack: React, Node.js, Firebase

🚀 Booster

Voice-controlled AI chatbot with context and natural language understanding.
Stack: Python, SpeechRecognition, GPT API

5. SKILLS SECTION

Categories (clean grid):

Languages

Python, JavaScript, HTML, CSS, Java (Basics)

Frameworks

Node.js, Express.js, Firebase

Tools

Git, GitHub, REST APIs

Research-Relevant Skills

Neural Computation
Timing-Based Models
Emotion-Aware Interaction
Cognitive Modeling Foundations

Soft Skills

Initiative, Problem-Solving, Consistency, Adaptability, Self-Learning

Foundations

Basic Linear Algebra
Basic Probability

6. CERTIFICATIONS SECTION

List as white cards:

Accenture Developer & Technology Experience

Google Cloud: Introduction to Generative AI

Oracle AI Foundations

Web Development Job Simulation (Moreton Bay)

Data Analytics Job Simulation (Deloitte)

International Computer Science Competition – Qualified

JLPT N5 – Ongoing

Each item gets a View Certificate button.

7. CONTACT SECTION

Light card form:

Name

Email

Message

Send Message (soft blue button)

Footer:
© 2025 Mahesh V. All rights reserved.

✔ FINAL NOTES FOR BUILDER

No


Primary theme: light mode only

Background: #FFFFFF or #FAFAFA

Accent colors: soft blue (#4A90E2) OR soft violet (#7A6EE6)

Secondary accents: light gray separators

Typography: modern sans-serif (Inter, Poppins, or SF Pro)

Cards: white with subtle shadow (0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.08))

Buttons: rounded, slight color accent

Hover effects: soft lift or subtle color shift

Overall vibe: clean, calm, bright, professional

Layout style: Apple-inspired minimalism

**Designing the Framework**

Okay, I'm starting to get a clear picture. The goal is a professional, academic portfolio for "Mahesh V." targeting professors and scholarship reviewers. The vibe needs to be clean, serious, and research-focused. I'm focusing on the website's structure and layout right now to ensure it conveys Mahesh's expertise effectively.


**Outlining Visual Style**

I've solidified the visual direction. It's a clean, professional, light-themed design, inspired by Apple's minimalist approach but tailored for academic contexts. I've pinned down the color palette (white/off-white background, soft blue/violet accents, dark/medium gray text) and basic card styling. Structure-wise, the core sections—Hero, About, Research Projects, Featured Projects, Skills, Certifications, and Contact—are set. Also, I've confirmed React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Lucide React, and Gemini API are the key tools.


**Structuring the Components**

I've got the component breakdown sorted now. I'm focusing on file organization and ensuring a modular structure: `index.html` with the CDN; `index.tsx` as the entry point; `App.tsx` handling the layout and routing; and specific components for each section, from the `Navbar` to the `Footer`. I've outlined data interfaces in `types.ts` and am planning on using a single-page, scrollable design based on the client's request.


**Fine-tuning the Layout**

I'm now zeroing in on the finer points of the layout. The core content container will have a maximum width for readability. Text will be styled to have a clean, "manuscript" feel. Research section will stand out with a subtle background variation and border/shadow. Project cards use a responsive grid with white backgrounds, subtle shadows, and a slight shadow increase on hover. Skills will be showcased with either small tags or clean list groups. The contact form remains simple; I'll mock the submission logic for now.


**Detailing Section Content**

My focus is now on populating each section with specific content and implementing interactions. I'm nailing down the hero constraints, with the headline locked in. The CerebroPulse Engine will be prominently featured within the research section, and certifications are being mapped to cards. Importantly, the "Research Projects" section, crucial for CSC considerations, is now prioritized. I'm incorporating the soft color requests, utilizing the client's preferred shade. Finally, I'm ensuring all animations are simple and professional, with gentle transitions on hover.


**Completing Core Functionality**

I've integrated all the components and finalized the core interactions.  The contact form is now fully rendered as UI-only.  I've double-checked all imports and TypeScript types for accuracy.  I've also fixed the styling for the contact form, and I've ensured a smooth scroll to the research section when the "View My Research" button is clicked.  I've incorporated the custom color palette within Tailwind, specifically for the colors requested by the client.  I'm now satisfied with the front-end design, and ready to refine final details.


**Implementing the Structure**

Okay, I've got the essential components nailed down now. I'm focusing on their specific functionalities. The `Navbar` gets a hamburger menu on smaller screens, and the "Research" section adopts a featured layout. "Projects" utilize a grid format, and skills are grouped logically. With these specific implementations in mind, I'm ready to begin generating the XML structure.
Here is the complete React code for the professional, academic-style portfolio website for Mahesh V.

entries.description: App metadata
Root HTML with Tailwind configuration for specific color requirements
Application Entry Point
TypeScript definitions
Reusable section title component for consistency
Navigation bar with smooth scroll
Hero section with clean academic aesthetic
About section with exact requested text
Research projects section with special emphasis
Standard featured projects grid
Categorized skills section
Certifications section
Contact form and footer
Simple footer
Main app component combining all sections

**Evaluating Placement Options**

I'm presently focusing on styling the social icons across the portfolio. My aim is to ensure the icons are unobtrusive yet readily accessible.


**Implementing Centered Contact**

I've decided to keep the contact section's layout centered. The existing `justify-center` on the parent div guides this decision.  I'll ensure the GitHub and LinkedIn icons are integrated harmoniously within this centered design, maintaining the requested aesthetic. I've also reconsidered modifying the navbar, opting to focus on Hero and Contact sections, following the established design. The GitHub URL is now set as `https://github.com/mahesh-v185`.


**Updating Portfolio Elements**

I'm now focusing on integrating the GitHub and LinkedIn links across the portfolio. I have reviewed the necessary files, specifically `Hero.tsx`, `Contact.tsx`, and `Footer.tsx`. The implementation appears straightforward, so I'll begin by updating each file to include the necessary import statements from `lucide-react` and modifying the layout to include the social media icons within each component. The initial changes are complete, and I am now proceeding with verifying the updated layout.


**Incorporating Icon Spacing**

I'm now determining how to space the social icons in the Hero Section. I'm leaning toward a row layout below the "View My Research" button, with a deliberate margin for visual clarity. I'm also adding the social icons within the Contact section's footer area, and updating the Footer's layout to accommodate the icons seamlessly. I have also fixed the GitHub link to be the user's profile and kept the example email address. I'm now implementing these adjustments.


**Updating Global Integration**

I'm now integrating the social icons into the portfolio's `Hero`, `Contact`, and `Footer` sections. I've reevaluated the Hero layout and opted to wrap the button and icons for a cohesive appearance. The Contact section will feature them alongside the email or below it for balance, and the Footer will offer global access. I've also double-checked all links and will implement these across all three sections for a consistent user experience. I'm focusing on ensuring these icons are elegant yet easily accessible.


**Completing Portfolio Integration**

I'm in the final phase of integrating the GitHub and LinkedIn links. I have successfully added the links to the `Contact` and `Footer` components. Now, I will implement those changes in the `Hero` section to ensure a consistent and user-friendly experience across the portfolio. The goal is a uniform appearance of these icons throughout the site.


**Implementing Hero Section**

I am now focusing on implementing the GitHub and LinkedIn links within the hero section, incorporating the previously decided button-icon layout. I've opted for a row below the "View My Research" button.  I've added the GitHub and LinkedIn icons as planned, and am finalizing the spacing to ensure optimal visual appeal and maintain the site's overall aesthetic consistency. I'm making sure that the icons are easily accessible.


**Refining Hero Implementation**

I'm presently integrating social icons into the Hero section using the provided code snippets. I'm focusing on ensuring proper styling, especially the hover effects and transitions. I've configured the links for the GitHub and LinkedIn icons as specified, and I am adjusting the layout to ensure they are visually balanced below the research button.


**Adjusting Form Behavior**

I'm now adjusting the form behavior for a better user experience. To ensure the form clears and offers feedback seamlessly, I've added state updates to set `isSubmitting` to false after a simulated API call, confirming the message was sent. I'm also implementing a timeout to automatically reset the submission confirmation after three seconds. I have also changed the form so it clears upon submission. This should provide users with immediate feedback and streamlined interaction.

**Adjusting Form Handling**

I'm presently optimizing the form to provide clear visual feedback after submission. I've integrated input validation to enhance the user experience and ensure required fields are filled before submission. After a successful simulated API call, the `isSubmitting` state is reset, and a message briefly appears confirming the submission. The form also clears automatically, and error messages are presented when input requirements aren't met. This streamlines the user experience.


**Optimizing Form Feedback**

I'm working on the form, specifically providing visual cues during submission. I've enhanced the user experience with validation, requiring all fields before submission, and added a simulated delay to represent a real API call. A success message now appears after a short delay and the form clears after successful submission, offering seamless interaction.

**Improving Form Feedback**

I'm now refining the form, concentrating on user feedback during the submission process. I'm adding input validation to improve the user experience and requiring complete data before submission. After a simulated API call, I display a success message and then clear the form automatically. The process aims to streamline the interaction.


**Enhancing Social Presence**

I'm presently updating the `Footer.tsx` file by integrating the social media links with a hover effect for a professional look. I am ensuring that the visual balance of elements is maintained. The GitHub and LinkedIn icons will be added seamlessly. I am also planning on adding the links to `Contact.tsx`.


**Enhancing Footer Integration**

I'm presently integrating social icons in the `Footer.tsx` file for a more polished look, using the given code. I'll add them after the "All Rights Reserved" text, and apply a hover effect to improve visibility and interaction. I am also working on adding these links to the `Contact.tsx` file.

**Expanding Certificate Data**

I'm currently focused on enriching the `Certifications.tsx` component. The plan is to enhance the `certifications` array by including certificate links. This involves populating the `link` property within the existing certificate objects, allowing for direct access to each certificate.


**Investigating Certificate Links**

I'm now investigating how to handle the "add links to certificates" request. Initially, I thought I had overlooked provided URLs. However, upon closer review, there are *no* explicit certificate URLs provided in the current or immediately visible history. I am now considering a few strategies: either making the UI ready for links, adding placeholder links, or checking for an implicit understanding of where these links might reside, given previous patterns in user prompts and data. I'll need to figure out what the best course of action is.


**Considering Link Strategies**

I am now focusing on the certificates. I realize I lack explicit URLs for the "add links" request.  Should I prompt the user for URLs, include placeholder links, or infer link locations? My immediate plan is to use placeholders, ensuring the "View Certificate" button leverages the `link` property. I've examined `Certifications.tsx` and can see the data structure needs a `link` property added. I'm leaning toward placeholder URLs for the moment, because they satisfy the user request, even though they are not ideal.


**Adding Certificate Link Logic**

I'm now revising the code to make the "View Certificate" button functional. Currently, it's just a button and doesn't lead anywhere.  I'll transform it into an anchor tag if a `link` property exists, and it's present for the certificate. I am going to implement the change to render the `<a>` tag only when the link exists. This is a robust solution to handle cases where links are not present.  I'll likely include placeholder links initially to illustrate where the URLs should be entered. This will let the user see the visual change.


**Implementing Placeholder Links**

I'm now integrating the certificate link logic into `Certifications.tsx`. I have reviewed `types.ts` and confirmed the `link?: string` property exists within the `Certification` interface. My plan is to include placeholder links, specifically "#", for each certificate in the array. This will make the "View Certificate" button functional, even if the provided links are not ideal. I'm focusing on ensuring the JSX renders an anchor tag (`<a>`) only when the `link` property is present, building in robustness for cases where links are not yet available.


**Prioritizing Placeholder Links**

I've just added the placeholder logic to `Certifications.tsx`, and I'm adding dummy links for each certificate to create an initial working UI. **Integrating Prototype Access**

I'm currently focused on incorporating the provided GitHub repository link into the "View Prototype" button within the `Research` component. I will need to make the necessary code modifications within `components/Research.tsx` to achieve this integration.


**Finalizing Button Linkage**

I've integrated the GitHub repository link into the "View Prototype" button. The button now successfully directs users to the code repository, completing the initial integration task. I believe it's ready for testing now.


**Enhancing Button Semantics**

I'm now changing the "View Prototype" button's HTML to an anchor tag for improved semantics.  Instead of using a button and handling `onClick`, I'm directly linking to the GitHub repository using an `<a>` tag with the appropriate `href` attribute. This includes adding `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"` for external links.  The component code will reflect this semantic change.


**Optimizing Data Integration**

I'm now integrating the GitHub repository link into `researchData` within `types.ts`. I've added a `link` property to the `ResearchProject` interface, then populated the corresponding object in `researchData` with the repository link.  This method provides a cleaner and more maintainable approach compared to hardcoding the link within the JSX.  This is a good step forward.


**Updating Button Behavior**

I'm now replacing the existing `button` element within the `Research` component with an `a` tag. I'll integrate the provided GitHub link, making it the `href` attribute. I'll also add `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"` for external link best practices. This should make the button behave more like a link and less like a standard button.

payload.text: Fit the size into Desktop size

**Revising Desktop Display**

I'm currently focused on improving the desktop display. Examining the codebase, I'm noting specific styling attributes in `Hero.tsx`, such as `min-h-screen`, and padding values. The `max-w-4xl` caught my eye as a potential area for adjustment. Next, I'll dive into `About.tsx` to identify further areas for desktop optimization.


**Evaluating Component Spacing**

I'm now analyzing the spacing and width usage across various components.  Specifically, `AcademicInterests`, `Research`, `Projects`, `Skills`, and `Certifications` employ `py-24`, while `Contact` has `py-24` and `max-w-2xl`. I'm concerned about the impact of the `min-h-screen` on Hero, even if this forces scrolling.


**Optimizing Desktop Presentation**

I'm now revising my plans to make this look better on a large screen. Reducing the existing vertical padding is crucial, so I'll standardize it to `py-16` or `py-12`. The Hero section's `min-h-screen` seems excessive; perhaps `min-h-[80vh]` or `py-20` is better. I will definitely reduce the padding surrounding the large heading text. For the About, Research, and Certifications sections, I will adjust the padding. Overall, this should vastly improve things.


**Improving Vertical Layout**

I'm now implementing the planned desktop improvements. I've adjusted the Hero's minimum height to `min-h-[85vh]` and slightly reduced the font size. I'm focusing on consistency: `About` is now `max-w-5xl`, and I'm updating other sections like `AcademicInterests` to `py-16` padding. I'm aiming for a harmonious, less-cramped feel. I will keep proceeding with the plan.


**Streamlining Component Padding**

I'm now revising the padding and widths across several components for a consistent visual flow. I'm swapping the `py-24` padding in `Projects`, `Skills`, and `Certifications` to `py-16`. Also, I'm updating `Certifications` from `max-w-4xl` to `max-w-5xl`. I'm also revising the `Contact` component, reducing padding to `py-16`. Finally, I'm reducing the bottom margin of section titles from `mb-12` to `mb-8`. Moreover, I'm examining the email in the `Contact` component, checking the mailto link against the user's provided address for accuracy.


**Correcting Contact Details**

I'm now revising the `Contact` component, reducing padding to `py-16` and also fixing the email address in the `mailto` link and displayed text, ensuring it matches the correct contact. I've also identified several files across components that need to be updated. I plan to reduce margins in `SectionTitle` from `mb-12` to `mb-10`.


**Generating Component Layout**

I'm now implementing the final changes to the component layout. I've adjusted the Hero section, revising the `min-h` to 85vh and modifying text sizing for better responsiveness.  The About section's padding is now `py-16`, with `max-w-5xl`.  `AcademicInterests`, `Research`, `Projects`, `Skills`, and `Certifications` use `py-16`, and `Research` now uses `max-w-6xl`. The email text in the Contact section is also fixed. I'm satisfied with the results, so I will produce the XML now.

To optimize the layout for desktop viewing, I will reduce the excessive vertical spacing (padding) across all sections, widen the content containers to utilize more screen width, and adjust the Hero section height so it doesn't force immediate scrolling.

I will also correct the email label in the contact section.
